That means the stock today offers about 25% upside, and about 8% annual returns.", "In this article, I'll share why investors might want to avoid car manufacturers like Ford, and how I came up with this valuation. I hope you enjoy, and I'll try to keep it clean and common sense.", "Even though the stock does offer some upside, I'd be very hesitant to invest in Ford, because the auto industry is not a great place for investors. The auto industry has many of the same problems for investors as the airline industry.", 'Auto manufacturers are capital intensive, requiring expensive factories to produce expensive products. These manufacturers also operate in a very competitive industry, where brands fight to stay in fashion and offer affordable prices.', "Warren Buffett tends to avoid companies that need to innovate to survive, not because he's old (even though he is), but because he's found that innovative companies are often forced to waste a lot of the capital that could be returned to shareholders.", 'Car manufacturers have to spend billions of dollars to innovate and maintain their expensive plants to keep up with fierce competition. Even though companies pour billions of dollars into capital expenditures to keep their business in tip-top shape, these capital expenditures end up canceling each other out, so companies never really move ahead.', "If one company institutes a ground-breaking change - the rest of the industry is forced to spend money to keep up. The effect ends up being that the company wasn't able to retain its advantage - and everyone else in the industry was forced to pay money to keep up. Unfortunately, this is a recurring cycle, and it will always be prevalent in the car industry unless one company can take a huge chunk of the market share.", "I don't think any auto company really has a moat, because there are many different car companies to buy from. All in all, I see this industry as a tough industry for shareholders to see high returns in.", 'I compared the S&P 500, Ford, General Motors (GM), Toyota (TM), Daimler (OTCPK:DDAIF), and CARZ - which is an ETF that tracks the auto manufacturing industry.', "Here's a look at the performance of these stocks/ETFs over the past 30 years:", 'Source: Seeking Alpha', 'The S&P 500, which ended up leading in total returns, rose nearly 1100% over the past 30 years. Toyota Motors has been the best-performing auto manufacturer, up over 600%.', 'CARZ returned about 100% or so over the past 10 years, which at about 7% annual returns is pretty good.', "Unfortunately, our hero Ford has returned about -32% since 1990. This number may not be entirely accurate because the past prices shown in the chart might not have been fully adjusted for stock splits, but Ford's historical performance has still been pretty unimpressive.", 'Why do these stocks underperform over the long term? Since auto manufacturers operate in a capital intensive, cyclical, price competitive industry, they really are never able to achieve a high return on invested capital.', 'Source: Author, Data from Morningstar', "Ford's averaged a return on equity of only about 9%, and averaged a return on capital of about 6% over the past 10 years. Companies that you'd want to invest in for the long-term should be able to maintain at least a 10% return on capital.", 'None of the car companies I looked at were averaging anywhere near a 10% ROIC, and they probably never will because of the competition in the industry.', "If you strictly invest for the long term, it wouldn't make sense to buy Ford. But if you're ok with investing in an undervalued company, and selling when it reaches fair value, then Ford might make a good investment at the right price.", "Now, it might seem like I hate Ford and that I wouldn't touch it with a 10-foot pole, but the truth is, I would consider investing in Ford as a short-term play. I definitely wouldn't consider Ford for a long term investment, but it could make sense as a short term recovery play.", 'I valued Ford using a Discounted Cash Flow model. This model projects what kind of cash flow Ford will create, and finds the fair price to pay for the stock today.', "I found Ford's weighted average cost of capital to be about 6%, but for the valuation, I used an 8% discount rate. I used a higher discount rate because there's a lot of uncertainty that comes with Ford's business because there's strong competition.", 'Source: Author', "Then, I projected the company's future revenue and future operating margins to estimate the company's future cash flows.", "I put a red box around some of the more important numbers, so it's easier for you to see what's going on in the valuation.", 'Source: Author', "I projected 16% revenue growth in 2022, and low revenue growth over the other years. Analysts at Yahoo Finance estimated that Ford will see about $130B in sales in 2021 and about $154B in sales in 2022 because 2022 is set to be a big year for Ford with Ford's release of the F-150 Lightning EV.", "Additionally, I projected 7% operating margins, because Ford has averaged 7% margins over the past 5 years, and they should be able to at least continue this trend going into the future with some of the cost efficiencies they've achieved.", "Taking all this into consideration, here's what the model estimates Ford to be worth:", 'Source: Author', 'Ford stock appears to be worth about $19 per share. That means we could expect to see at least 8% annual returns if the stock reaches fair value.', 'Additionally, I listed 2 price points where it might be more attractive to pick up shares. At $9 per share, we could expect to see at least 16% returns if the stock reaches fair value, and at $7 per share, we could expect to see 22% annual returns.', "I wouldn't feel comfortable investing in Ford unless it dropped below $7 per share. I wish I had done this analysis earlier when Ford was trading at $5 a share! That would have been cool.", "Even though Ford might not be the best stock to hold for the long term, it does offer some upside as a recovery play. The stock's fair value seems to be about $19 today, and it offers about 8% annual returns.", 'Thank you very much for reading, and I hope you have a great rest of your day.', 'This article was written by', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I/we have no positions in any stocks mentioned, and no plans to initiate any positions within the next 72 hours.  I wrote this article myself, and it expresses my own opinions. I am not receiving compensation for it (other than from Seeking Alpha). I have no business relationship with any company whose stock is mentioned in this article. ', "Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Title: Ford: Not Worth The Risk 
 Content: ["At today's prices, Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) seems to offer average returns. Investing in the auto industry seems like a dangerous game, but Ford makes sense at the right price.", 'Source: Author', "Today, Ford's fair value appears to be about $19 per share. That means the stock today offers about 25% upside, and about 8% annual returns.", "In this article, I'll share why investors might want to avoid car manufacturers like Ford, and how I came up with this valuation. I hope you enjoy, and I'll try to keep it clean and common sense.", "Even though the stock does offer some upside, I'd be very hesitant to invest in Ford, because the auto industry is not a great place for investors. The auto industry has many of the same problems for investors as the airline industry.", 'Auto manufacturers are capital intensive, requiring expensive factories to produce expensive products. These manufacturers also operate in a very competitive industry, where brands fight to stay in fashion and offer affordable prices.', "Warren Buffett tends to avoid companies that need to innovate to survive, not because he's old (even though he is), but because he's found that innovative companies are often forced to waste a lot of the capital that could be returned to shareholders.", 'Car manufacturers have to spend billions of dollars to innovate and maintain their expensive plants to keep up with fierce competition. Even though companies pour billions of dollars into capital expenditures to keep their business in tip-top shape, these capital expenditures end up canceling each other out, so companies never really move ahead.', "If one company institutes a ground-breaking change - the rest of the industry is forced to spend money to keep up. The effect ends up being that the company wasn't able to retain its advantage - and everyone else in the industry was forced to pay money to keep up. Unfortunately, this is a recurring cycle, and it will always be prevalent in the car industry unless one company can take a huge chunk of the market share.", "I don't think any auto company really has a moat, because there are many different car companies to buy from. All in all, I see this industry as a tough industry for shareholders to see high returns in.", 'I compared the S&P 500, Ford, General Motors (GM), Toyota (TM), Daimler (OTCPK:DDAIF), and CARZ - which is an ETF that tracks the auto manufacturing industry.', "Here's a look at the performance of these stocks/ETFs over the past 30 years:", 'Source: Seeking Alpha', 'The S&P 500, which ended up leading in total returns, rose nearly 1100% over the past 30 years. Toyota Motors has been the best-performing auto manufacturer, up over 600%.', 'CARZ returned about 100% or so over the past 10 years, which at about 7% annual returns is pretty good.', "Unfortunately, our hero Ford has returned about -32% since 1990. This number may not be entirely accurate because the past prices shown in the chart might not have been fully adjusted for stock splits, but Ford's historical performance has still been pretty unimpressive.", 'Why do these stocks underperform over the long term? Since auto manufacturers operate in a capital intensive, cyclical, price competitive industry, they really are never able to achieve a high return on invested capital.', 'Source: Author, Data from Morningstar', "Ford's averaged a return on equity of only about 9%, and averaged a return on capital of about 6% over the past 10 years. Companies that you'd want to invest in for the long-term should be able to maintain at least a 10% return on capital.", 'None of the car companies I looked at were averaging anywhere near a 10% ROIC, and they probably never will because of the competition in the industry.', "If you strictly invest for the long term, it wouldn't make sense to buy Ford. But if you're ok with investing in an undervalued company, and selling when it reaches fair value, then Ford might make a good investment at the right price.", "Now, it might seem like I hate Ford and that I wouldn't touch it with a 10-foot pole, but the truth is, I would consider investing in Ford as a short-term play. I definitely wouldn't consider Ford for a long term investment, but it could make sense as a short term recovery play.", 'I valued Ford using a Discounted Cash Flow model. This model projects what kind of cash flow Ford will create, and finds the fair price to pay for the stock today.', "I found Ford's weighted average cost of capital to be about 6%, but for the valuation, I used an 8% discount rate. I used a higher discount rate because there's a lot of uncertainty that comes with Ford's business because there's strong competition.", 'Source: Author', "Then, I projected the company's future revenue and future operating margins to estimate the company's future cash flows.", "I put a red box around some of the more important numbers, so it's easier for you to see what's going on in the valuation.", 'Source: Author', "I projected 16% revenue growth in 2022, and low revenue growth over the other years. Analysts at Yahoo Finance estimated that Ford will see about $130B in sales in 2021 and about $154B in sales in 2022 because 2022 is set to be a big year for Ford with Ford's release of the F-150 Lightning EV.", "Additionally, I projected 7% operating margins, because Ford has averaged 7% margins over the past 5 years, and they should be able to at least continue this trend going into the future with some of the cost efficiencies they've achieved.", "Taking all this into consideration, here's what the model estimates Ford to be worth:", 'Source: Author', 'Ford stock appears to be worth about $19 per share. That means we could expect to see at least 8% annual returns if the stock reaches fair value.', 'Additionally, I listed 2 price points where it might be more attractive to pick up shares. At $9 per share, we could expect to see at least 16% returns if the stock reaches fair value, and at $7 per share, we could expect to see 22% annual returns.', "I wouldn't feel comfortable investing in Ford unless it dropped below $7 per share. I wish I had done this analysis earlier when Ford was trading at $5 a share! That would have been cool.", "Even though Ford might not be the best stock to hold for the long term, it does offer some upside as a recovery play. The stock's fair value seems to be about $19 today, and it offers about 8% annual returns.", 'Thank you very much for reading, and I hope you have a great rest of your day.', 'This article was written by', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I/we have no positions in any stocks mentioned, and no plans to initiate any positions within the next 72 hours.  I wrote this article myself, and it expresses my own opinions. I am not receiving compensation for it (other than from Seeking Alpha). Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.", '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '']

These manufacturers also operate in a very competitive industry, where brands fight to stay in fashion and offer affordable prices.', "Warren Buffett tends to avoid companies that need to innovate to survive, not because he's old (even though he is), but because he's found that innovative companies are often forced to waste a lot of the capital that could be returned to shareholders.", 'Car manufacturers have to spend billions of dollars to innovate and maintain their expensive plants to keep up with fierce competition. Even though companies pour billions of dollars into capital expenditures to keep their business in tip-top shape, these capital expenditures end up canceling each other out, so companies never really move ahead.', "If one company institutes a ground-breaking change - the rest of the industry is forced to spend money to keep up. The effect ends up being that the company wasn't able to retain its advantage - and everyone else in the industry was forced to pay money to keep up. Unfortunately, this is a recurring cycle, and it will always be prevalent in the car industry unless one company can take a huge chunk of the market share.", "I don't think any auto company really has a moat, because there are many different car companies to buy from. All in all, I see this industry as a tough industry for shareholders to see high returns in.", 'I compared the S&P 500, Ford, General Motors (GM), Toyota (TM), Daimler (OTCPK:DDAIF), and CARZ - which is an ETF that tracks the auto manufacturing industry.', "Here's a look at the performance of these stocks/ETFs over the past 30 years:", 'Source: Seeking Alpha', 'The S&P 500, which ended up leading in total returns, rose nearly 1100% over the past 30 years. Toyota Motors has been the best-performing auto manufacturer, up over 600%.', 'CARZ returned about 100% or so over the past 10 years, which at about 7% annual returns is pretty good.', "Unfortunately, our hero Ford has returned about -32% since 1990. This number may not be entirely accurate because the past prices shown in the chart might not have been fully adjusted for stock splits, but Ford's historical performance has still been pretty unimpressive.", 'Why do these stocks underperform over the long term? Title: Ford: Not Worth The Risk 
 Content: ["At today's prices, Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) seems to offer average returns. Investing in the auto industry seems like a dangerous game, but Ford makes sense at the right price.", 'Source: Author', "Today, Ford's fair value appears to be about $19 per share. That means the stock today offers about 25% upside, and about 8% annual returns.", "In this article, I'll share why investors might want to avoid car manufacturers like Ford, and how I came up with this valuation. I hope you enjoy, and I'll try to keep it clean and common sense.", "Even though the stock does offer some upside, I'd be very hesitant to invest in Ford, because the auto industry is not a great place for investors. The auto industry has many of the same problems for investors as the airline industry.", 'Auto manufacturers are capital intensive, requiring expensive factories to produce expensive products. These manufacturers also operate in a very competitive industry, where brands fight to stay in fashion and offer affordable prices.', "Warren Buffett tends to avoid companies that need to innovate to survive, not because he's old (even though he is), but because he's found that innovative companies are often forced to waste a lot of the capital that could be returned to shareholders.", 'Car manufacturers have to spend billions of dollars to innovate and maintain their expensive plants to keep up with fierce competition. Even though companies pour billions of dollars into capital expenditures to keep their business in tip-top shape, these capital expenditures end up canceling each other out, so companies never really move ahead.', "If one company institutes a ground-breaking change - the rest of the industry is forced to spend money to keep up. The effect ends up being that the company wasn't able to retain its advantage - and everyone else in the industry was forced to pay money to keep up. Unfortunately, this is a recurring cycle, and it will always be prevalent in the car industry unless one company can take a huge chunk of the market share.", "I don't think any auto company really has a moat, because there are many different car companies to buy from. All in all, I see this industry as a tough industry for shareholders to see high returns in.", 'I compared the S&P 500, Ford, General Motors (GM), Toyota (TM), Daimler (OTCPK:DDAIF), and CARZ - which is an ETF that tracks the auto manufacturing industry.', "Here's a look at the performance of these stocks/ETFs over the past 30 years:", 'Source: Seeking Alpha', 'The S&P 500, which ended up leading in total returns, rose nearly 1100% over the past 30 years. Toyota Motors has been the best-performing auto manufacturer, up over 600%.', 'CARZ returned about 100% or so over the past 10 years, which at about 7% annual returns is pretty good.', "Unfortunately, our hero Ford has returned about -32% since 1990. This number may not be entirely accurate because the past prices shown in the chart might not have been fully adjusted for stock splits, but Ford's historical performance has still been pretty unimpressive.", 'Why do these stocks underperform over the long term? Since auto manufacturers operate in a capital intensive, cyclical, price competitive industry, they really are never able to achieve a high return on invested capital.', 'Source: Author, Data from Morningstar', "Ford's averaged a return on equity of only about 9%, and averaged a return on capital of about 6% over the past 10 years. Companies that you'd want to invest in for the long-term should be able to maintain at least a 10% return on capital.", 'None of the car companies I looked at were averaging anywhere near a 10% ROIC, and they probably never will because of the competition in the industry.', "If you strictly invest for the long term, it wouldn't make sense to buy Ford. But if you're ok with investing in an undervalued company, and selling when it reaches fair value, then Ford might make a good investment at the right price.", "Now, it might seem like I hate Ford and that I wouldn't touch it with a 10-foot pole, but the truth is, I would consider investing in Ford as a short-term play. I definitely wouldn't consider Ford for a long term investment, but it could make sense as a short term recovery play.", 'I valued Ford using a Discounted Cash Flow model. This model projects what kind of cash flow Ford will create, and finds the fair price to pay for the stock today.', "I found Ford's weighted average cost of capital to be about 6%, but for the valuation, I used an 8% discount rate. I used a higher discount rate because there's a lot of uncertainty that comes with Ford's business because there's strong competition.", 'Source: Author', "Then, I projected the company's future revenue and future operating margins to estimate the company's future cash flows.", "I put a red box around some of the more important numbers, so it's easier for you to see what's going on in the valuation.", 'Source: Author', "I projected 16% revenue growth in 2022, and low revenue growth over the other years. Analysts at Yahoo Finance estimated that Ford will see about $130B in sales in 2021 and about $154B in sales in 2022 because 2022 is set to be a big year for Ford with Ford's release of the F-150 Lightning EV.", "Additionally, I projected 7% operating margins, because Ford has averaged 7% margins over the past 5 years, and they should be able to at least continue this trend going into the future with some of the cost efficiencies they've achieved.", "Taking all this into consideration, here's what the model estimates Ford to be worth:", 'Source: Author', 'Ford stock appears to be worth about $19 per share. That means we could expect to see at least 8% annual returns if the stock reaches fair value.', 'Additionally, I listed 2 price points where it might be more attractive to pick up shares. At $9 per share, we could expect to see at least 16% returns if the stock reaches fair value, and at $7 per share, we could expect to see 22% annual returns.', "I wouldn't feel comfortable investing in Ford unless it dropped below $7 per share. I wish I had done this analysis earlier when Ford was trading at $5 a share! That would have been cool.", "Even though Ford might not be the best stock to hold for the long term, it does offer some upside as a recovery play. The stock's fair value seems to be about $19 today, and it offers about 8% annual returns.", 'Thank you very much for reading, and I hope you have a great rest of your day.', 'This article was written by', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I/we have no positions in any stocks mentioned, and no plans to initiate any positions within the next 72 hours. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.", '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '']

The auto industry has many of the same problems for investors as the airline industry.", 'Auto manufacturers are capital intensive, requiring expensive factories to produce expensive products. These manufacturers also operate in a very competitive industry, where brands fight to stay in fashion and offer affordable prices.', "Warren Buffett tends to avoid companies that need to innovate to survive, not because he's old (even though he is), but because he's found that innovative companies are often forced to waste a lot of the capital that could be returned to shareholders.", 'Car manufacturers have to spend billions of dollars to innovate and maintain their expensive plants to keep up with fierce competition. Even though companies pour billions of dollars into capital expenditures to keep their business in tip-top shape, these capital expenditures end up canceling each other out, so companies never really move ahead.', "If one company institutes a ground-breaking change - the rest of the industry is forced to spend money to keep up. The effect ends up being that the company wasn't able to retain its advantage - and everyone else in the industry was forced to pay money to keep up. Unfortunately, this is a recurring cycle, and it will always be prevalent in the car industry unless one company can take a huge chunk of the market share.", "I don't think any auto company really has a moat, because there are many different car companies to buy from. Toyota Motors has been the best-performing auto manufacturer, up over 600%.', 'CARZ returned about 100% or so over the past 10 years, which at about 7% annual returns is pretty good.', "Unfortunately, our hero Ford has returned about -32% since 1990. This number may not be entirely accurate because the past prices shown in the chart might not have been fully adjusted for stock splits, but Ford's historical performance has still been pretty unimpressive.", 'Why do these stocks underperform over the long term? That means we could expect to see at least 8% annual returns if the stock reaches fair value.', 'Additionally, I listed 2 price points where it might be more attractive to pick up shares. I wish I had done this analysis earlier when Ford was trading at $5 a share! That would have been cool.", "Even though Ford might not be the best stock to hold for the long term, it does offer some upside as a recovery play. The stock's fair value seems to be about $19 today, and it offers about 8% annual returns.", 'Thank you very much for reading, and I hope you have a great rest of your day.', 'This article was written by', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I/we have no positions in any stocks mentioned, and no plans to initiate any positions within the next 72 hours.  Content: ["At today's prices, Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) seems to offer average returns. Investing in the auto industry seems like a dangerous game, but Ford makes sense at the right price.", 'Source: Author', "Today, Ford's fair value appears to be about $19 per share. That means the stock today offers about 25% upside, and about 8% annual returns.", "In this article, I'll share why investors might want to avoid car manufacturers like Ford, and how I came up with this valuation. I hope you enjoy, and I'll try to keep it clean and common sense.", "Even though the stock does offer some upside, I'd be very hesitant to invest in Ford, because the auto industry is not a great place for investors. The auto industry has many of the same problems for investors as the airline industry.", 'Auto manufacturers are capital intensive, requiring expensive factories to produce expensive products. These manufacturers also operate in a very competitive industry, where brands fight to stay in fashion and offer affordable prices.', "Warren Buffett tends to avoid companies that need to innovate to survive, not because he's old (even though he is), but because he's found that innovative companies are often forced to waste a lot of the capital that could be returned to shareholders.", 'Car manufacturers have to spend billions of dollars to innovate and maintain their expensive plants to keep up with fierce competition. Even though companies pour billions of dollars into capital expenditures to keep their business in tip-top shape, these capital expenditures end up canceling each other out, so companies never really move ahead.', "If one company institutes a ground-breaking change - the rest of the industry is forced to spend money to keep up. The effect ends up being that the company wasn't able to retain its advantage - and everyone else in the industry was forced to pay money to keep up. Unfortunately, this is a recurring cycle, and it will always be prevalent in the car industry unless one company can take a huge chunk of the market share.", "I don't think any auto company really has a moat, because there are many different car companies to buy from. All in all, I see this industry as a tough industry for shareholders to see high returns in.", 'I compared the S&P 500, Ford, General Motors (GM), Toyota (TM), Daimler (OTCPK:DDAIF), and CARZ - which is an ETF that tracks the auto manufacturing industry.', "Here's a look at the performance of these stocks/ETFs over the past 30 years:", 'Source: Seeking Alpha', 'The S&P 500, which ended up leading in total returns, rose nearly 1100% over the past 30 years. Toyota Motors has been the best-performing auto manufacturer, up over 600%.', 'CARZ returned about 100% or so over the past 10 years, which at about 7% annual returns is pretty good.', "Unfortunately, our hero Ford has returned about -32% since 1990. This number may not be entirely accurate because the past prices shown in the chart might not have been fully adjusted for stock splits, but Ford's historical performance has still been pretty unimpressive.", 'Why do these stocks underperform over the long term? Since auto manufacturers operate in a capital intensive, cyclical, price competitive industry, they really are never able to achieve a high return on invested capital.', 'Source: Author, Data from Morningstar', "Ford's averaged a return on equity of only about 9%, and averaged a return on capital of about 6% over the past 10 years. Companies that you'd want to invest in for the long-term should be able to maintain at least a 10% return on capital.", 'None of the car companies I looked at were averaging anywhere near a 10% ROIC, and they probably never will because of the competition in the industry.', "If you strictly invest for the long term, it wouldn't make sense to buy Ford. But if you're ok with investing in an undervalued company, and selling when it reaches fair value, then Ford might make a good investment at the right price.", "Now, it might seem like I hate Ford and that I wouldn't touch it with a 10-foot pole, but the truth is, I would consider investing in Ford as a short-term play. I definitely wouldn't consider Ford for a long term investment, but it could make sense as a short term recovery play.", 'I valued Ford using a Discounted Cash Flow model. This model projects what kind of cash flow Ford will create, and finds the fair price to pay for the stock today.', "I found Ford's weighted average cost of capital to be about 6%, but for the valuation, I used an 8% discount rate. I used a higher discount rate because there's a lot of uncertainty that comes with Ford's business because there's strong competition.", 'Source: Author', "Then, I projected the company's future revenue and future operating margins to estimate the company's future cash flows.", "I put a red box around some of the more important numbers, so it's easier for you to see what's going on in the valuation.", 'Source: Author', "I projected 16% revenue growth in 2022, and low revenue growth over the other years. Analysts at Yahoo Finance estimated that Ford will see about $130B in sales in 2021 and about $154B in sales in 2022 because 2022 is set to be a big year for Ford with Ford's release of the F-150 Lightning EV.", "Additionally, I projected 7% operating margins, because Ford has averaged 7% margins over the past 5 years, and they should be able to at least continue this trend going into the future with some of the cost efficiencies they've achieved.", "Taking all this into consideration, here's what the model estimates Ford to be worth:", 'Source: Author', 'Ford stock appears to be worth about $19 per share. That means we could expect to see at least 8% annual returns if the stock reaches fair value.', 'Additionally, I listed 2 price points where it might be more attractive to pick up shares. At $9 per share, we could expect to see at least 16% returns if the stock reaches fair value, and at $7 per share, we could expect to see 22% annual returns.', "I wouldn't feel comfortable investing in Ford unless it dropped below $7 per share. I wish I had done this analysis earlier when Ford was trading at $5 a share! That would have been cool.", "Even though Ford might not be the best stock to hold for the long term, it does offer some upside as a recovery play. The stock's fair value seems to be about $19 today, and it offers about 8% annual returns.", 'Thank you very much for reading, and I hope you have a great rest of your day.', 'This article was written by', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I/we have no positions in any stocks mentioned, and no plans to initiate any positions within the next 72 hours.  I wrote this article myself, and it expresses my own opinions. I am not receiving compensation for it (other than from Seeking Alpha). I have no business relationship with any company whose stock is mentioned in this article. ', "Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.", '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '']

Investing in the auto industry seems like a dangerous game, but Ford makes sense at the right price.", 'Source: Author', "Today, Ford's fair value appears to be about $19 per share. That means the stock today offers about 25% upside, and about 8% annual returns.", "In this article, I'll share why investors might want to avoid car manufacturers like Ford, and how I came up with this valuation. I hope you enjoy, and I'll try to keep it clean and common sense.", "Even though the stock does offer some upside, I'd be very hesitant to invest in Ford, because the auto industry is not a great place for investors. The auto industry has many of the same problems for investors as the airline industry.", 'Auto manufacturers are capital intensive, requiring expensive factories to produce expensive products. These manufacturers also operate in a very competitive industry, where brands fight to stay in fashion and offer affordable prices.', "Warren Buffett tends to avoid companies that need to innovate to survive, not because he's old (even though he is), but because he's found that innovative companies are often forced to waste a lot of the capital that could be returned to shareholders.", 'Car manufacturers have to spend billions of dollars to innovate and maintain their expensive plants to keep up with fierce competition. Even though companies pour billions of dollars into capital expenditures to keep their business in tip-top shape, these capital expenditures end up canceling each other out, so companies never really move ahead.', "If one company institutes a ground-breaking change - the rest of the industry is forced to spend money to keep up. The effect ends up being that the company wasn't able to retain its advantage - and everyone else in the industry was forced to pay money to keep up. Unfortunately, this is a recurring cycle, and it will always be prevalent in the car industry unless one company can take a huge chunk of the market share.", "I don't think any auto company really has a moat, because there are many different car companies to buy from. All in all, I see this industry as a tough industry for shareholders to see high returns in.", 'I compared the S&P 500, Ford, General Motors (GM), Toyota (TM), Daimler (OTCPK:DDAIF), and CARZ - which is an ETF that tracks the auto manufacturing industry.', "Here's a look at the performance of these stocks/ETFs over the past 30 years:", 'Source: Seeking Alpha', 'The S&P 500, which ended up leading in total returns, rose nearly 1100% over the past 30 years. Toyota Motors has been the best-performing auto manufacturer, up over 600%.', 'CARZ returned about 100% or so over the past 10 years, which at about 7% annual returns is pretty good.', "Unfortunately, our hero Ford has returned about -32% since 1990. This number may not be entirely accurate because the past prices shown in the chart might not have been fully adjusted for stock splits, but Ford's historical performance has still been pretty unimpressive.", 'Why do these stocks underperform over the long term? Since auto manufacturers operate in a capital intensive, cyclical, price competitive industry, they really are never able to achieve a high return on invested capital.', 'Source: Author, Data from Morningstar', "Ford's averaged a return on equity of only about 9%, and averaged a return on capital of about 6% over the past 10 years. Companies that you'd want to invest in for the long-term should be able to maintain at least a 10% return on capital.", 'None of the car companies I looked at were averaging anywhere near a 10% ROIC, and they probably never will because of the competition in the industry.', "If you strictly invest for the long term, it wouldn't make sense to buy Ford. But if you're ok with investing in an undervalued company, and selling when it reaches fair value, then Ford might make a good investment at the right price.", "Now, it might seem like I hate Ford and that I wouldn't touch it with a 10-foot pole, but the truth is, I would consider investing in Ford as a short-term play. That means the stock today offers about 25% upside, and about 8% annual returns.", "In this article, I'll share why investors might want to avoid car manufacturers like Ford, and how I came up with this valuation. I hope you enjoy, and I'll try to keep it clean and common sense.", "Even though the stock does offer some upside, I'd be very hesitant to invest in Ford, because the auto industry is not a great place for investors. The auto industry has many of the same problems for investors as the airline industry.", 'Auto manufacturers are capital intensive, requiring expensive factories to produce expensive products. These manufacturers also operate in a very competitive industry, where brands fight to stay in fashion and offer affordable prices.', "Warren Buffett tends to avoid companies that need to innovate to survive, not because he's old (even though he is), but because he's found that innovative companies are often forced to waste a lot of the capital that could be returned to shareholders.", 'Car manufacturers have to spend billions of dollars to innovate and maintain their expensive plants to keep up with fierce competition. Even though companies pour billions of dollars into capital expenditures to keep their business in tip-top shape, these capital expenditures end up canceling each other out, so companies never really move ahead.', "If one company institutes a ground-breaking change - the rest of the industry is forced to spend money to keep up. The effect ends up being that the company wasn't able to retain its advantage - and everyone else in the industry was forced to pay money to keep up. Unfortunately, this is a recurring cycle, and it will always be prevalent in the car industry unless one company can take a huge chunk of the market share.", "I don't think any auto company really has a moat, because there are many different car companies to buy from. All in all, I see this industry as a tough industry for shareholders to see high returns in.", 'I compared the S&P 500, Ford, General Motors (GM), Toyota (TM), Daimler (OTCPK:DDAIF), and CARZ - which is an ETF that tracks the auto manufacturing industry.', "Here's a look at the performance of these stocks/ETFs over the past 30 years:", 'Source: Seeking Alpha', 'The S&P 500, which ended up leading in total returns, rose nearly 1100% over the past 30 years. Toyota Motors has been the best-performing auto manufacturer, up over 600%.', 'CARZ returned about 100% or so over the past 10 years, which at about 7% annual returns is pretty good.', "Unfortunately, our hero Ford has returned about -32% since 1990. This number may not be entirely accurate because the past prices shown in the chart might not have been fully adjusted for stock splits, but Ford's historical performance has still been pretty unimpressive.", 'Why do these stocks underperform over the long term? Since auto manufacturers operate in a capital intensive, cyclical, price competitive industry, they really are never able to achieve a high return on invested capital.', 'Source: Author, Data from Morningstar', "Ford's averaged a return on equity of only about 9%, and averaged a return on capital of about 6% over the past 10 years. Companies that you'd want to invest in for the long-term should be able to maintain at least a 10% return on capital.", 'None of the car companies I looked at were averaging anywhere near a 10% ROIC, and they probably never will because of the competition in the industry.', "If you strictly invest for the long term, it wouldn't make sense to buy Ford. But if you're ok with investing in an undervalued company, and selling when it reaches fair value, then Ford might make a good investment at the right price.", "Now, it might seem like I hate Ford and that I wouldn't touch it with a 10-foot pole, but the truth is, I would consider investing in Ford as a short-term play. I definitely wouldn't consider Ford for a long term investment, but it could make sense as a short term recovery play.", 'I valued Ford using a Discounted Cash Flow model. This model projects what kind of cash flow Ford will create, and finds the fair price to pay for the stock today.', "I found Ford's weighted average cost of capital to be about 6%, but for the valuation, I used an 8% discount rate. I used a higher discount rate because there's a lot of uncertainty that comes with Ford's business because there's strong competition.", 'Source: Author', "Then, I projected the company's future revenue and future operating margins to estimate the company's future cash flows.", "I put a red box around some of the more important numbers, so it's easier for you to see what's going on in the valuation.", 'Source: Author', "I projected 16% revenue growth in 2022, and low revenue growth over the other years. Analysts at Yahoo Finance estimated that Ford will see about $130B in sales in 2021 and about $154B in sales in 2022 because 2022 is set to be a big year for Ford with Ford's release of the F-150 Lightning EV.", "Additionally, I projected 7% operating margins, because Ford has averaged 7% margins over the past 5 years, and they should be able to at least continue this trend going into the future with some of the cost efficiencies they've achieved.", "Taking all this into consideration, here's what the model estimates Ford to be worth:", 'Source: Author', 'Ford stock appears to be worth about $19 per share. That means we could expect to see at least 8% annual returns if the stock reaches fair value.', 'Additionally, I listed 2 price points where it might be more attractive to pick up shares. At $9 per share, we could expect to see at least 16% returns if the stock reaches fair value, and at $7 per share, we could expect to see 22% annual returns.', "I wouldn't feel comfortable investing in Ford unless it dropped below $7 per share. I wish I had done this analysis earlier when Ford was trading at $5 a share! That would have been cool.", "Even though Ford might not be the best stock to hold for the long term, it does offer some upside as a recovery play. The stock's fair value seems to be about $19 today, and it offers about 8% annual returns.", 'Thank you very much for reading, and I hope you have a great rest of your day.', 'This article was written by', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I/we have no positions in any stocks mentioned, and no plans to initiate any positions within the next 72 hours.  I wrote this article myself, and it expresses my own opinions. I am not receiving compensation for it (other than from Seeking Alpha). I have no business relationship with any company whose stock is mentioned in this article. ', "Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Title: Ford: Not Worth The Risk 
 Content: ["At today's prices, Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) seems to offer average returns. Investing in the auto industry seems like a dangerous game, but Ford makes sense at the right price.", 'Source: Author', "Today, Ford's fair value appears to be about $19 per share. That means the stock today offers about 25% upside, and about 8% annual returns.", "In this article, I'll share why investors might want to avoid car manufacturers like Ford, and how I came up with this valuation. I hope you enjoy, and I'll try to keep it clean and common sense.", "Even though the stock does offer some upside, I'd be very hesitant to invest in Ford, because the auto industry is not a great place for investors. The auto industry has many of the same problems for investors as the airline industry.", 'Auto manufacturers are capital intensive, requiring expensive factories to produce expensive products. These manufacturers also operate in a very competitive industry, where brands fight to stay in fashion and offer affordable prices.', "Warren Buffett tends to avoid companies that need to innovate to survive, not because he's old (even though he is), but because he's found that innovative companies are often forced to waste a lot of the capital that could be returned to shareholders.", 'Car manufacturers have to spend billions of dollars to innovate and maintain their expensive plants to keep up with fierce competition. Even though companies pour billions of dollars into capital expenditures to keep their business in tip-top shape, these capital expenditures end up canceling each other out, so companies never really move ahead.', "If one company institutes a ground-breaking change - the rest of the industry is forced to spend money to keep up. The effect ends up being that the company wasn't able to retain its advantage - and everyone else in the industry was forced to pay money to keep up. Unfortunately, this is a recurring cycle, and it will always be prevalent in the car industry unless one company can take a huge chunk of the market share.", "I don't think any auto company really has a moat, because there are many different car companies to buy from. All in all, I see this industry as a tough industry for shareholders to see high returns in.", 'I compared the S&P 500, Ford, General Motors (GM), Toyota (TM), Daimler (OTCPK:DDAIF), and CARZ - which is an ETF that tracks the auto manufacturing industry.', "Here's a look at the performance of these stocks/ETFs over the past 30 years:", 'Source: Seeking Alpha', 'The S&P 500, which ended up leading in total returns, rose nearly 1100% over the past 30 years. Toyota Motors has been the best-performing auto manufacturer, up over 600%.', 'CARZ returned about 100% or so over the past 10 years, which at about 7% annual returns is pretty good.', "Unfortunately, our hero Ford has returned about -32% since 1990. This number may not be entirely accurate because the past prices shown in the chart might not have been fully adjusted for stock splits, but Ford's historical performance has still been pretty unimpressive.", 'Why do these stocks underperform over the long term? Since auto manufacturers operate in a capital intensive, cyclical, price competitive industry, they really are never able to achieve a high return on invested capital.', 'Source: Author, Data from Morningstar', "Ford's averaged a return on equity of only about 9%, and averaged a return on capital of about 6% over the past 10 years. Companies that you'd want to invest in for the long-term should be able to maintain at least a 10% return on capital.", 'None of the car companies I looked at were averaging anywhere near a 10% ROIC, and they probably never will because of the competition in the industry.', "If you strictly invest for the long term, it wouldn't make sense to buy Ford. But if you're ok with investing in an undervalued company, and selling when it reaches fair value, then Ford might make a good investment at the right price.", "Now, it might seem like I hate Ford and that I wouldn't touch it with a 10-foot pole, but the truth is, I would consider investing in Ford as a short-term play. I definitely wouldn't consider Ford for a long term investment, but it could make sense as a short term recovery play.", 'I valued Ford using a Discounted Cash Flow model. This model projects what kind of cash flow Ford will create, and finds the fair price to pay for the stock today.', "I found Ford's weighted average cost of capital to be about 6%, but for the valuation, I used an 8% discount rate. I used a higher discount rate because there's a lot of uncertainty that comes with Ford's business because there's strong competition.", 'Source: Author', "Then, I projected the company's future revenue and future operating margins to estimate the company's future cash flows.", "I put a red box around some of the more important numbers, so it's easier for you to see what's going on in the valuation.", 'Source: Author', "I projected 16% revenue growth in 2022, and low revenue growth over the other years. Analysts at Yahoo Finance estimated that Ford will see about $130B in sales in 2021 and about $154B in sales in 2022 because 2022 is set to be a big year for Ford with Ford's release of the F-150 Lightning EV.", "Additionally, I projected 7% operating margins, because Ford has averaged 7% margins over the past 5 years, and they should be able to at least continue this trend going into the future with some of the cost efficiencies they've achieved.", "Taking all this into consideration, here's what the model estimates Ford to be worth:", 'Source: Author', 'Ford stock appears to be worth about $19 per share. That means we could expect to see at least 8% annual returns if the stock reaches fair value.', 'Additionally, I listed 2 price points where it might be more attractive to pick up shares. At $9 per share, we could expect to see at least 16% returns if the stock reaches fair value, and at $7 per share, we could expect to see 22% annual returns.', "I wouldn't feel comfortable investing in Ford unless it dropped below $7 per share. I wish I had done this analysis earlier when Ford was trading at $5 a share! That would have been cool.", "Even though Ford might not be the best stock to hold for the long term, it does offer some upside as a recovery play. The stock's fair value seems to be about $19 today, and it offers about 8% annual returns.", 'Thank you very much for reading, and I hope you have a great rest of your day.', 'This article was written by', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I/we have no positions in any stocks mentioned, and no plans to initiate any positions within the next 72 hours.  I wrote this article myself, and it expresses my own opinions. I am not receiving compensation for it (other than from Seeking Alpha). Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.", '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '']

These manufacturers also operate in a very competitive industry, where brands fight to stay in fashion and offer affordable prices.', "Warren Buffett tends to avoid companies that need to innovate to survive, not because he's old (even though he is), but because he's found that innovative companies are often forced to waste a lot of the capital that could be returned to shareholders.", 'Car manufacturers have to spend billions of dollars to innovate and maintain their expensive plants to keep up with fierce competition. Even though companies pour billions of dollars into capital expenditures to keep their business in tip-top shape, these capital expenditures end up canceling each other out, so companies never really move ahead.', "If one company institutes a ground-breaking change - the rest of the industry is forced to spend money to keep up. The effect ends up being that the company wasn't able to retain its advantage - and everyone else in the industry was forced to pay money to keep up. Unfortunately, this is a recurring cycle, and it will always be prevalent in the car industry unless one company can take a huge chunk of the market share.", "I don't think any auto company really has a moat, because there are many different car companies to buy from. All in all, I see this industry as a tough industry for shareholders to see high returns in.", 'I compared the S&P 500, Ford, General Motors (GM), Toyota (TM), Daimler (OTCPK:DDAIF), and CARZ - which is an ETF that tracks the auto manufacturing industry.', "Here's a look at the performance of these stocks/ETFs over the past 30 years:", 'Source: Seeking Alpha', 'The S&P 500, which ended up leading in total returns, rose nearly 1100% over the past 30 years. Toyota Motors has been the best-performing auto manufacturer, up over 600%.', 'CARZ returned about 100% or so over the past 10 years, which at about 7% annual returns is pretty good.', "Unfortunately, our hero Ford has returned about -32% since 1990. This number may not be entirely accurate because the past prices shown in the chart might not have been fully adjusted for stock splits, but Ford's historical performance has still been pretty unimpressive.", 'Why do these stocks underperform over the long term? Title: Ford: Not Worth The Risk 
 Content: ["At today's prices, Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) seems to offer average returns. Investing in the auto industry seems like a dangerous game, but Ford makes sense at the right price.", 'Source: Author', "Today, Ford's fair value appears to be about $19 per share. That means the stock today offers about 25% upside, and about 8% annual returns.", "In this article, I'll share why investors might want to avoid car manufacturers like Ford, and how I came up with this valuation. I hope you enjoy, and I'll try to keep it clean and common sense.", "Even though the stock does offer some upside, I'd be very hesitant to invest in Ford, because the auto industry is not a great place for investors. The auto industry has many of the same problems for investors as the airline industry.", 'Auto manufacturers are capital intensive, requiring expensive factories to produce expensive products. These manufacturers also operate in a very competitive industry, where brands fight to stay in fashion and offer affordable prices.', "Warren Buffett tends to avoid companies that need to innovate to survive, not because he's old (even though he is), but because he's found that innovative companies are often forced to waste a lot of the capital that could be returned to shareholders.", 'Car manufacturers have to spend billions of dollars to innovate and maintain their expensive plants to keep up with fierce competition. Even though companies pour billions of dollars into capital expenditures to keep their business in tip-top shape, these capital expenditures end up canceling each other out, so companies never really move ahead.', "If one company institutes a ground-breaking change - the rest of the industry is forced to spend money to keep up. The effect ends up being that the company wasn't able to retain its advantage - and everyone else in the industry was forced to pay money to keep up. Unfortunately, this is a recurring cycle, and it will always be prevalent in the car industry unless one company can take a huge chunk of the market share.", "I don't think any auto company really has a moat, because there are many different car companies to buy from. All in all, I see this industry as a tough industry for shareholders to see high returns in.", 'I compared the S&P 500, Ford, General Motors (GM), Toyota (TM), Daimler (OTCPK:DDAIF), and CARZ - which is an ETF that tracks the auto manufacturing industry.', "Here's a look at the performance of these stocks/ETFs over the past 30 years:", 'Source: Seeking Alpha', 'The S&P 500, which ended up leading in total returns, rose nearly 1100% over the past 30 years. Toyota Motors has been the best-performing auto manufacturer, up over 600%.', 'CARZ returned about 100% or so over the past 10 years, which at about 7% annual returns is pretty good.', "Unfortunately, our hero Ford has returned about -32% since 1990. This number may not be entirely accurate because the past prices shown in the chart might not have been fully adjusted for stock splits, but Ford's historical performance has still been pretty unimpressive.", 'Why do these stocks underperform over the long term? Since auto manufacturers operate in a capital intensive, cyclical, price competitive industry, they really are never able to achieve a high return on invested capital.', 'Source: Author, Data from Morningstar', "Ford's averaged a return on equity of only about 9%, and averaged a return on capital of about 6% over the past 10 years. Companies that you'd want to invest in for the long-term should be able to maintain at least a 10% return on capital.", 'None of the car companies I looked at were averaging anywhere near a 10% ROIC, and they probably never will because of the competition in the industry.', "If you strictly invest for the long term, it wouldn't make sense to buy Ford. But if you're ok with investing in an undervalued company, and selling when it reaches fair value, then Ford might make a good investment at the right price.", "Now, it might seem like I hate Ford and that I wouldn't touch it with a 10-foot pole, but the truth is, I would consider investing in Ford as a short-term play. I definitely wouldn't consider Ford for a long term investment, but it could make sense as a short term recovery play.", 'I valued Ford using a Discounted Cash Flow model. This model projects what kind of cash flow Ford will create, and finds the fair price to pay for the stock today.', "I found Ford's weighted average cost of capital to be about 6%, but for the valuation, I used an 8% discount rate. I used a higher discount rate because there's a lot of uncertainty that comes with Ford's business because there's strong competition.", 'Source: Author', "Then, I projected the company's future revenue and future operating margins to estimate the company's future cash flows.", "I put a red box around some of the more important numbers, so it's easier for you to see what's going on in the valuation.", 'Source: Author', "I projected 16% revenue growth in 2022, and low revenue growth over the other years. Analysts at Yahoo Finance estimated that Ford will see about $130B in sales in 2021 and about $154B in sales in 2022 because 2022 is set to be a big year for Ford with Ford's release of the F-150 Lightning EV.", "Additionally, I projected 7% operating margins, because Ford has averaged 7% margins over the past 5 years, and they should be able to at least continue this trend going into the future with some of the cost efficiencies they've achieved.", "Taking all this into consideration, here's what the model estimates Ford to be worth:", 'Source: Author', 'Ford stock appears to be worth about $19 per share. That means we could expect to see at least 8% annual returns if the stock reaches fair value.', 'Additionally, I listed 2 price points where it might be more attractive to pick up shares. At $9 per share, we could expect to see at least 16% returns if the stock reaches fair value, and at $7 per share, we could expect to see 22% annual returns.', "I wouldn't feel comfortable investing in Ford unless it dropped below $7 per share. I wish I had done this analysis earlier when Ford was trading at $5 a share! That would have been cool.", "Even though Ford might not be the best stock to hold for the long term, it does offer some upside as a recovery play. The stock's fair value seems to be about $19 today, and it offers about 8% annual returns.", 'Thank you very much for reading, and I hope you have a great rest of your day.', 'This article was written by', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I/we have no positions in any stocks mentioned, and no plans to initiate any positions within the next 72 hours. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.", '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '', '']

Title: Ford: Not Worth The Risk 
 Content: ["At today's prices, Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) seems to offer average returns. Investing in the auto industry seems like a dangerous game, but Ford makes sense at the right price.", 'Source: Author', "Today, Ford's fair value appears to be about $19 per share. That means the stock today offers about 25% upside, and about 8% annual returns.", "In this article, I'll share why investors might want to avoid car manufacturers like Ford, and how I came up with this valuation. I hope you enjoy, and I'll try to keep it clean and common sense.", "Even though the stock does offer some upside, I'd be very hesitant to invest in Ford, because the auto industry is not a great place for investors. The auto industry has many of the same problems for investors as the airline industry.", 'Auto manufacturers are capital intensive, requiring expensive factories to produce expensive products. These manufacturers also operate in a very competitive industry, where brands fight to stay in fashion and offer affordable prices.', "Warren Buffett tends to avoid companies that need to innovate to survive, not because he's old (even though he is), but because he's found that innovative companies are often forced to waste a lot of the capital that could be returned to shareholders.", 'Car manufacturers have to spend billions of dollars to innovate and maintain their expensive plants to keep up with fierce competition. Even though companies pour billions of dollars into capital expenditures to keep their business in tip-top shape, these capital expenditures end up canceling each other out, so companies never really move ahead.', "If one company institutes a ground-breaking change - the rest of the industry is forced to spend money to keep up. The effect ends up being that the company wasn't able to retain its advantage - and everyone else in the industry was forced to pay money to keep up. Unfortunately, this is a recurring cycle, and it will always be prevalent in the car industry unless one company can take a huge chunk of the market share.", "I don't think any auto company really has a moat, because there are many different car companies to buy from. All in all, I see this industry as a tough industry for shareholders to see high returns in.", 'I compared the S&P 500, Ford, General Motors (GM), Toyota (TM), Daimler (OTCPK:DDAIF), and CARZ - which is an ETF that tracks the auto manufacturing industry.', "Here's a look at the performance of these stocks/ETFs over the past 30 years:", 'Source: Seeking Alpha', 'The S&P 500, which ended up leading in total returns, rose nearly 1100% over the past 30 years. Toyota Motors has been the best-performing auto manufacturer, up over 600%.', 'CARZ returned about 100% or so over the past 10 years, which at about 7% annual returns is pretty good.', "Unfortunately, our hero Ford has returned about -32% since 1990. This number may not be entirely accurate because the past prices shown in the chart might not have been fully adjusted for stock splits, but Ford's historical performance has still been pretty unimpressive.", 'Why do these stocks underperform over the long term? Since auto manufacturers operate in a capital intensive, cyclical, price competitive industry, they really are never able to achieve a high return on invested capital.', 'Source: Author, Data from Morningstar', "Ford's averaged a return on equity of only about 9%, and averaged a return on capital of about 6% over the past 10 years. Companies that you'd want to invest in for the long-term should be able to maintain at least a 10% return on capital.", 'None of the car companies I looked at were averaging anywhere near a 10% ROIC, and they probably never will because of the competition in the industry.', "If you strictly invest for the long term, it wouldn't make sense to buy Ford. But if you're ok with investing in an undervalued company, and selling when it reaches fair value, then Ford might make a good investment at the right price.", "Now, it might seem like I hate Ford and that I wouldn't touch it with a 10-foot pole, but the truth is, I would consider investing in Ford as a short-term play. I definitely wouldn't consider Ford for a long term investment, but it could make sense as a short term recovery play.", 'I valued Ford using a Discounted Cash Flow model. This model projects what kind of cash flow Ford will create, and finds the fair price to pay for the stock today.', "I found Ford's weighted average cost of capital to be about 6%, but for the valuation, I used an 8% discount rate. I used a higher discount rate because there's a lot of uncertainty that comes with Ford's business because there's strong competition.", 'Source: Author', "Then, I projected the company's future revenue and future operating margins to estimate the company's future cash flows.", "I put a red box around some of the more important numbers, so it's easier for you to see what's going on in the valuation.", 'Source: Author', "I projected 16% revenue growth in 2022, and low revenue growth over the other years. Analysts at Yahoo Finance estimated that Ford will see about $130B in sales in 2021 and about $154B in sales in 2022 because 2022 is set to be a big year for Ford with Ford's release of the F-150 Lightning EV.", "Additionally, I projected 7% operating margins, because Ford has averaged 7% margins over the past 5 years, and they should be able to at least continue this trend going into the future with some of the cost efficiencies they've achieved.", "Taking all this into consideration, here's what the model estimates Ford to be worth:", 'Source: Author', 'Ford stock appears to be worth about $19 per share. That means we could expect to see at least 8% annual returns if the stock reaches fair value.', 'Additionally, I listed 2 price points where it might be more attractive to pick up shares. At $9 per share, we could expect to see at least 16% returns if the stock reaches fair value, and at $7 per share, we could expect to see 22% annual returns.', "I wouldn't feel comfortable investing in Ford unless it dropped below $7 per share. I wish I had done this analysis earlier when Ford was trading at $5 a share! That would have been cool.", "Even though Ford might not be the best stock to hold for the long term, it does offer some upside as a recovery play. The stock's fair value seems to be about $19 today, and it offers about 8% annual returns.", 'Thank you very much for reading, and I hope you have a great rest of your day.', 'This article was written by', 'Analystâ\x80\x99s Disclosure: I/we have no positions in any stocks mentioned, and no plans to initiate any positions within the next 72 hours.
